name: data-store
title: Choose the primary data store
description: Pick the primary persistent store. Drives schema discipline, query model, scaling characteristics, and ops burden.

starter.summary: Decide the primary persistence layer for the project.
starter.issue: The data model is sticky — switching stores after launch is expensive. Picking the right shape (relational vs document vs KV) and the right vendor (self-hosted vs managed) early matters.
assumptions: Workload shape (read-heavy vs write-heavy, OLTP vs OLAP) is understood.
Backup/restore and migration tolerances are known.
Team has comfort with at least one of the candidates.

positions.title: PostgreSQL (self-hosted or managed)
positions.description: Relational. JSONB support gives flexibility when needed.

positions.title: SQLite (embedded or with Litestream/Turso)
positions.description: File-based SQL. Modern variants (Turso, libSQL) support replication.

positions.title: Managed Postgres (Supabase / Neon / RDS)
positions.description: PostgreSQL as a service. No ops, more $$.

implications: Migration strategy (e.g., Drizzle, Prisma, raw SQL, ORM choice) follows.
Backup and DR playbooks need to be specified.
Local dev story needs an answer (Docker postgres? sqlite file? mocked?).

notes_for_agent: Ask whether the data is structured/relational or sparse/varying. Ask about expected scale (rows, queries/sec). If the project is a single-tenant local-first tool, SQLite is usually right. If it's a multi-tenant web app, managed Postgres is usually right. Don't offer NoSQL unless the data really warrants it.
